help.translation: A vast forested belt sprawls to the northwest of the imperial capital, Zebrudia.
At the end of a narrow, half-game-trail of a path that had been forced through the forest, where many wild monsters dwell, sat the Treasury in question.
help.note: 件の is a literary "the said / aforementioned," reaching back to a referent established earlier (the chapter title in this case). 半ば獣道のような = "halfway a beast-track" — 半ば (half / partway) softens the comparison.

help.translation: ── 【White Wolves' Den】.
help.note: ── opens the line as a beat-pause — a common literary flourish for introducing a name in narration. 体言止め: the line ends on a noun.

help.translation: It was originally the territory of a large pack of 'Silvermoons,' a species of wolf native to the great forest, with fur resembling the gleam of a silver moon.
help.note: 〜だったという = "it is said (that it was) ~" — という marks the line as established lore rather than the narrator's direct claim. 縄張りとする一帯 = "the area (they) treat as their territory."

help.translation: Strong limbs that could move at speed through the gaps between trees on uneven forest ground; fur that turned away every kind of offensive magic.
Reapers of the forest — fangs that could crush a trained Hunter's body, the wits to wield magic (small in effect though it was), and packs large enough to bring down prey even above their own weight.
help.note: 効果は小さいながらも = "small in effect, but still…" — the concessive ながらも flags the contrast with what comes after. すら in 自分よりも格上の魔物をすら狩る emphasises "even (prey) above their own grade." The whole sentence is a long noun phrase ending on 森の死神 ("reapers of the forest") — 体言止め as a verdict.

help.translation: Yet for all that the Silvermoons were known as fearsome, no-easy-fight monsters, they had just two real weaknesses.
help.note: 一筋縄ではいかない = idiom, literally "cannot be handled with a single rope" — i.e. tough to deal with. しかし inserted mid-sentence (after に は) flips the line into contrast at exactly the point where the contrast hinges.

help.translation: A small body that, even fully grown, only reached about a meter.
help.note: 〜にしかならない = "only ends up being ~" — しか〜ない ("only") attached to the result of なる pins the smallness as a hard ceiling, not just a tendency.

help.translation: And — moon-coloured fur, the source of their name, at which no one could keep from staring.
help.note: 〜ずにはいられない is a literary fixed pattern: "cannot help (doing) ~ / cannot keep from ~." 見惚れずにはいられない = "cannot help being transfixed." The whole sentence is a 体言止め: it ends on the noun 毛皮.

help.translation: Their bones, their fangs, and above all their fur — the Silvermoons' organic materials boasted some of the highest value of any monster's.
help.note: 屈指 = "ranking among the very best," literary register. 〜を誇る = "to take pride in / to boast (a quality)," used as a flat statement of merit, not just emotion.

help.translation: So much so — to a degree that rivalled the spoils of risking a Treasury raid.
help.note: それこそ is an emphatic intensifier: "that's exactly the kind of (degree)…" The line ends on 程に with no main verb (体言止め variant), letting the comparison hang as a flourish.

help.translation: And so, the Silvermoons became the prey of every Hunter who came calling.
help.note: 故に is the literary equivalent of だから / そのため ("therefore"), more formal and weightier than the colloquial connector.

help.translation: These monsters had wisdom, strength, numbers — every one of those — but to Hunters who possessed greater numbers, greater strength, and greater wisdom still, they were ideal prey.
help.note: 知恵を、力を、数を、その全てを — listing-with-を across four parallel items ("wisdom, strength, numbers, all of it") in a rhetorical drumbeat. 格好の獲物 = "prime / ideal prey," a stock idiom.

help.translation: Monsters are living creatures.
No matter how powerful they may have been, they do not simply arise on their own the way the Phantoms haunting Treasuries do.
help.note: どれほど〜としても = "no matter how (much) ~." 〜たりはしない puts は inside 〜たりしない to give the negation contrastive force: "it is not the case that they (do anything like) spontaneously appear."

help.translation: And so, before long, the wolves were hunted into oblivion.
help.note: かくして is literary "thus, in this way." 狩り尽くす compounds 狩る ("hunt") with 尽くす ("do exhaustively") — "hunt to the last one." The passive 〜尽くされた frames the wolves as the recipients of that exhaustive hunt.

help.translation: The Silvermoons dwindled in number as if in inverse proportion to the imperial capital's growth, and that very fact, in turn, drove the rarity of their fur higher still.
help.note: 〜かのように = "as if ~"; the か introduces a hedged comparison the narrator isn't fully committing to. その事実がまた = "that fact, in turn," with また marking another step in the chain of consequences.

help.translation: Where once it was deemed a creature to be wary of when entering the forest, the Silvermoon was demoted to the rank of a 'lucky encounter' if one was found at all.
help.note: 〜べきとされた = "was deemed something one ought to ~." 貶められた literally "was lowered / debased" — a strong verb for an animal's loss of stature, framing rarity as a kind of indignity.

help.translation: And by the time the imperial capital had come to be called the Hunters' holy land, the Silvermoons had left only the traces of their once-large packs in the sprawling network of dens, and vanished entirely from view.
help.note: 名残 = "lingering vestiges" — what's left behind of something already gone. その姿を完全に消した = "erased their figure entirely," with 消す used as "vanish from sight," not "kill."

help.translation: 【White Wolves' Den】.
help.note: 体言止め — a single-noun line that pulls the camera back from history onto the chapter's location. Mirrors p003 to bookend the historical aside.

help.translation: It was only in the past decade or so that wolves with their bodies drenched in blood began to be rumoured spawning in dens that should have stood empty.
help.note: ようになった = "came to (be / do) ~" — marks a gradual change of state. 噂されるようになった layers a passive 噂される ("be rumoured") with that gradual-change marker, a typical pattern for hearsay that builds over time. はず after 誰もいなくなった signals a departure from expectation: "the dens that ought to have been empty."
